title: The Null Key Funnel
short_description: Null and default join keys are collapsing many records into one bad partition.
business_context: A fraud-scoring Spark job joins click events to account metadata. Suddenly one task runs far longer than the others because a huge spike of invalid account keys all funnel into the same default join bucket.
problem_statement: Use the sample events to identify records with invalid account keys that should be routed out of the main join path instead of being forced through a default key.
student_task: Write a single read-only SQL query that returns the records with null or default account keys and labels the validation failure reason.
learning_goals: Recognize bad join-key quality as a cause of skew.
Route invalid records away from the main distributed join path.
Treat default keys as data-quality signals, not normal business values.
broken_code:
SELECT COALESCE(account_id, 0) AS safe_account_id, *
FROM click_events;
production_logs: 2026-06-12 12:17:44 WARN one shuffle partition is 19x larger than median
solution_query:
SELECT
  event_id,
  account_id,
  country,
  spend,
  CASE
    WHEN account_id IS NULL THEN 'null_account_id'
    ELSE 'default_account_id'
  END AS validation_reason
FROM click_events
WHERE account_id IS NULL OR account_id IN (0, -1)
explanation: Replacing invalid keys with a default bucket hides the data-quality problem and often creates severe skew. The safer approach is to isolate those bad records so the main join path handles only valid business keys.
common_mistakes: Coalescing null keys to zero and pretending the records are now safe to join.
Returning only null keys and forgetting that default sentinels such as 0 or -1 are also invalid.
Fixing the skew symptom without addressing the underlying data-quality problem.
hints: Not every bad key is literally NULL.
The business problem is to identify and route bad records, not to repair them in place.
A CASE expression can label why each row is invalid.
